old_claim.note: Mercantilism Economic Theory provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: The corrected node is anchored to 15th-century oceanic exploration and route-finding; mercantilism is later commercial policy context, not an enabling prerequisite for the start of the exploration capability.

source_shape.source_locator: Article body and top questions: mercantilism was prevalent in the Western world from the 16th to 18th century and common in Europe from the 16th to 18th century.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Mercantilism is a 16th- to 18th-century economic system and policy context, not a direct prerequisite for the 15th-century opening of oceanic exploration.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The edge inverted chronology and mixed motive/policy context into an operational navigation node.

ontology_before: The graph treated a later economic-policy framework as a direct enabling prerequisite for an earlier exploration capability.
ontology_after: The graph keeps mercantilism available for downstream colonial, trade, and economic-system nodes without making it a direct prerequisite for oceanic route-finding.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Graph invariant 2026-06-20-exploration-global-oceanic-scope keeps mercantilism_economic_theory absent as a direct prerequisite.
would_reject_if: The node were rescoped to mature mercantilist colonial policy rather than oceanic exploration.
A source showed formal mercantilist theory was necessary before early modern European oceanic route-finding began.
short_reason: Mercantilism is later policy context, not a direct prerequisite for the 15th-century exploration node.
